explanation: A code smell where methods accept multiple boolean parameters, creating call sites that are impossible to read without checking the method signature, prone to argument transposition bugs.

clues: The domain objects are just data containers and all the business logic lives in service classes
The Order class has 20 getters and setters but no methods that express business rules
Business rules are scattered across multiple service classes instead of living on the domain objects they operate on
The domain model looks like a database schema with no behavior, and the service layer contains all the interesting logic
Domain objects contain only data fields and accessors, while service classes implement all business rules and invariants that should belong to the domain

A domain model where objects are pure data holders with no behavior, and all business logic is placed in procedural service classes, violating the principle that objects should encapsulate both data and behavior
explanation: An anti-pattern where domain objects contain only data with no behavior, and all business logic lives in separate service classes, missing the benefits of object-oriented encapsulation.

clues: The code is incredibly complex, and when asked why, the author says it's 'for performance'
A custom caching layer was built before measuring whether there was actually a performance problem
The developer replaced readable LINQ queries with hand-optimized loops, but profiling shows the code path accounts for 0.1% of execution time
Weeks were spent optimizing a function that profiling revealed is never in the hot path, while the actual bottleneck remains unaddressed
Complex, hard-to-maintain code was written to optimize a theoretical performance concern that was never validated by measurement or profiling
Code complexity was introduced to optimize performance before profiling identified an actual bottleneck, sacrificing readability and maintainability for a theoretical gain that may never materialize
explanation: The practice of making code more complex to optimize performance before measuring or profiling has identified an actual bottleneck, trading maintainability for unvalidated theoretical gains.

clues: Users of the API keep needing to understand implementation details to use it correctly
The ORM abstraction breaks down and you end up writing raw SQL to work around its limitations
The file-like network socket abstraction fails because network connections can silently drop, unlike local files
The 'transparent' remote procedure call abstraction leaks because network latency and partial failures behave nothing like local function calls
The abstraction hides the underlying complexity most of the time, but edge cases force the caller to understand and work around the implementation details
An abstraction that fails to fully hide its underlying complexity, forcing users to understand implementation details in edge cases that the abstraction doesn't properly handle
explanation: An abstraction that works well in simple cases but breaks down in edge cases, forcing users to understand the underlying implementation details it was supposed to hide.

clues: Changing one module always seems to break another seemingly unrelated module
The unit test for Class A requires instantiating and configuring Classes B, C, and D
The two modules share internal data structures directly instead of communicating through a stable interface
Modifying the internal representation of the User class requires changes in the OrderService, ReportGenerator, and EmailSender
Components are intertwined through shared mutable state, direct field access, and concrete class dependencies instead of interfaces
Components are so interdependent through shared internal details, concrete implementations, and mutable state that changing one requires changes to many others
explanation: A design problem where components are excessively interdependent through shared internal details, making it impossible to change one component without affecting many others.

clues: The code is indented so deeply that it runs off the right side of the screen
Error handling is nearly impossible because each nested callback has its own separate error path
The asynchronous logic is nested five callbacks deep, making the control flow unreadable
Each step of the workflow is a callback inside a callback inside a callback, forming a pyramid of doom
The code would be far more readable using promises, async/await, or another abstraction instead of deeply nested callbacks
Deeply nested asynchronous callback functions forming a pyramid structure that makes control flow, error handling, and debugging extremely difficult
explanation: An anti-pattern where deeply nested asynchronous callbacks create code that is hard to read, maintain, and debug, often called the 'pyramid of doom.'

clues: Tests pass when run individually but fail when run as a suite in a specific order
The test results are non-deterministic and depend on which tests ran before them
One test modifies global state that another test depends on, but the state is never reset between tests
Test A sets a static configuration value and doesn't clean it up, causing Test B to fail because it assumes the default configuration
Shared mutable state modified by one test and not reset between test runs causes order-dependent test failures
Tests that modify shared mutable state without proper setup and teardown cause order-dependent failures, where tests pass in isolation but fail when run together in certain sequences
explanation: When tests modify shared state without proper cleanup, causing subsequent tests to fail depending on execution order, making the test suite unreliable and non-deterministic.

clues: Disk space on the logging servers fills up during incidents, right when we need logs the most
The service writes so many log lines that the logging pipeline falls behind and starts dropping entries
Error handling code logs the full stack trace at ERROR level inside a retry loop, generating thousands of identical log entries per second
A single error condition inside a hot loop generates 10,000 identical log lines per second, overwhelming the logging infrastructure
Verbose logging in high-throughput code paths produces log volumes that saturate the disk, network, or logging pipeline, paradoxically degrading observability during incidents
Excessive logging in error paths or hot loops produces log volumes that overwhelm the logging infrastructure, filling disks and dropping log entries precisely when observability is most needed
explanation: When excessive logging during error conditions or high-throughput code paths overwhelms the logging infrastructure, paradoxically reducing observability during the incidents when logs are most valuable.

clues: Two services are both hanging, waiting for each other to respond
The request trace shows Service A waiting on Service B, and Service B waiting on Service A
Neither service can make progress because each holds a resource the other needs
Service A holds a lock on the user table and waits for Service B to release the order table, while Service B holds the order table lock and waits for Service A
Cross-service synchronous calls form a circular dependency where each service holds a resource and waits for a resource held by another
A deadlock spanning multiple distributed services where circular dependencies in resource acquisition cause all involved services to block indefinitely, each waiting for a resource held by another
explanation: A deadlock that spans multiple distributed services, where circular dependencies in resource acquisition cause all involved parties to wait indefinitely for each other.

clues: Users are seeing outdated information, but the database has the correct data
The product price was updated hours ago, but some users still see the old price
Cache invalidation logic has a bug that leaves expired entries in the cache layer
The CDN is serving a cached version of the page from before the content update, and the TTL hasn't expired yet
The application serves data from a cache layer that was not invalidated or refreshed after the underlying data changed
A caching layer continues to serve stale data after the source of truth has been updated, because cache invalidation or TTL management failed to trigger a refresh
explanation: A reliability issue where cached data becomes stale after the underlying data source is updated, but the cache continues serving outdated values due to failed invalidation or excessive TTL.
